19.32: Berzelai the Gileadite came down from Rogelim and crossed over to the king at the Jordan to accompany him to the river.
19.33: Berzelai was very old, eighty years old, he had provided food to the king during his stay in Mahanaim, for he was a very rich man.
19.34: The king said to Berzelai, "Come with me, I will feed you at my house in Jerusalem."«
19.35: But Berzelai answered the king, "How many more years must I live, that I should go up with the king to Jerusalem?"
19.36: I am now eighty years old. Can I distinguish between good and evil? Can your servant still taste what he eats and drinks? Can I still hear the voices of singers, both men and women? And why should your servant still be a burden to my lord the king?
19.37: Your servant will go a little way beyond the Jordan with the king. And why would the king grant me this reward?
19.38: »Please let your servant return home so that I may die in my own city, near the tomb of my father and mother. But here is your servant Shamaam; let him go with my lord the king, and do for him whatever seems best to you.”
19.39: The king said, "Let Shamaam come with me, and I will do for him whatever you please, and I will grant you whatever you desire from me."«
19.40: And when all the people had crossed the Jordan, the king also crossed it, and the king kissed Berzelai and blessed him, and he returned home.
19.41: The king went to Gilgal and Shamaam went with him and all the people of Judah, as well as half of the people of Israel escorted the king.
19.42: But then all the men of Israel came to the king and said to him, «Why have our brothers, the men of Judah, taken you away and brought the king, his household, and all of David’s men across the Jordan?»
19.43: All the men of Judah answered the men of Israel, «It is because the king has a closer relationship with me; why are you angry about this? Have we lived at the king’s expense? Or have we received anything from him?»
19.44: The men of Israel answered the men of Judah, saying, «I have ten shares in the king, and David belongs to me more than to you. Why have you insulted me like this? Was not my word the first to restore my king?” And the men of Judah spoke harsher than the men of Israel.

20.1: There happened to be a man of Belial, named Sheba, son of Bochri, a Benjamite; he blew his trumpet and said, «We have no portion in David, nor do we have an inheritance among the sons of Jesse. Every man to his own tents, O Israel.»
20.2: And all the men of Israel turned away from David and followed Sheba, son of Bochri. But the men of Judah rallied to their king, from the Jordan to Jerusalem.
20.3: David returned to his house in Jerusalem, and the king took the ten concubines he had left to look after the house and put them in a guarded house. He provided for their upkeep, but he did not visit them again, and they were confined until the day of their death, living in a state of widowhood.
20.4: The king said to Amasa, "Summon the men of Judah to me within three days, and you yourself must be present here."«
20.5: Amasa went to summon Judah, but he delayed beyond the time the king had set.
20.6: Then David said to Abishai, «Sheba son of Bochri is now going to do us more harm than Absalom. You, therefore, take your master’s servants and pursue him, lest he find fortified cities and escape from our sight.»
20.7: Behind Abishai went Joab’s men, the Cherethites and the Phelethites and all the valiant men; they went out from Jerusalem to pursue Sheba, son of Bochri.
20.8: When they approached the great stone at Gibeon, Amasa came before them. Joab was wearing a military tunic, and over the tunic was a sword in its scabbard, fastened at his waist. As he advanced, the sword fell out.
20.9: And Joab said to Amasa, «Are you well, my brother?» And Joab’s right hand grasped Amasa’s beard to kiss him.
20.10: Amasa did not notice the sword in Joab's hand, and Joab struck him in the stomach, spilling his entrails on the ground. Amasa died without a second blow. Joab and his brother Abishai then pursued Sheba, son of Bochri.
20.11: But one of Joab's young men stayed with Amasa and said, "Whoever is favorable to Joab and whoever is for David, let him follow Joab."«
20.12: Now Amasa had rolled in his blood in the middle of the road. This man, seeing that all the people stopped, pulled Amasa out of the road into a field and threw a cloak over him, because he saw that all those who came near him stopped.
20.13: When he was removed from the road, everyone passed on after Joab, in pursuit of Sheba, son of Bochri.
20.14: Joab went through all the tribes of Israel as far as Abel and Beth-Maacha, and all the elite men gathered and followed him.
20.15: They came to besiege Sheba in Abel-Beth-Maahah and they built a siege ramp against the city, which reached the rampart, and all the people who were with Joab tried to bring down the wall.
20.16: Then a wise woman began to call out from the city: «Listen, listen, I beg you. Tell Joab: Come here, I want to speak with you.»
20.17: He approached her and the woman said, "Are you Joab?" He replied, "I am." And she said to him, "Listen to the words of your servant." He replied, "I will listen."«
20.18: And she said: "In the old days, people used to say: 'Consult Abel,' and everything was settled that way.
20.19: I am one of the peaceful and faithful cities in Israel, yet you seek to destroy a city that is a mother in Israel. Why would you destroy the Lord's inheritance?»
20.20: Joab replied: «Far, far from me. I do not want to destroy or ruin?”
20.21: "That's not how it is. But a man from the hill country of Ephraim, named Sheba, son of Bochri, has raised his hand against King David. Hand him over, him alone, and I will leave the city." The woman said to Joab, "Here, his head will be thrown over the wall to you."»
20.22: The woman went to all the people and spoke wisely to them, and they cut off the head of Sheba, son of Bochri, and threw it to Joab. Joab sounded the trumpet, and the people scattered from the city, each to their tents, and Joab returned to Jerusalem to the king.
20.23: Joab commanded the entire army of Israel, Benaiah son of Jehoiada commanded the Cherethites and the Phelethites,
20.24: Aduram was in charge of forced labor, Jehoshaphat, son of Ahilud, was the recorder, Shiva was the secretary,
20.26: And Ira the Jairite was also a close advisor to David.

21.1: In the days of David, there was a famine, and it lasted for three consecutive years. David inquired of the Lord, and the Lord said, «It is because of Saul and his household, because there is bloodshed, because he has killed some of the Gibeonites.»
21.2: The king summoned the Gibeonites and said to them, “The Gibeonites were not of the Israelites, but were of the Amorites, and the Israelites had sworn an oath to them. Nevertheless, Saul had wanted to strike them down out of zeal for the Israelites and Judah.”.
21.3: David said to the Gibeonites, «What shall I do for you, and with what shall I make atonement, so that you may bless the inheritance of the Lord?»
21.4: The Gibeonites said to him, «It is not a matter of silver and gold for us with Saul and his household, nor is it our intention to kill anyone in Israel.» And the king said, «What then do you want me to do for you?»
21.5: They answered the king: «This man has destroyed us and had planned to exterminate us, so that we would not survive in all the territory of Israel:
21.6: "Give us seven men from among his sons, so that we may hang them before the Lord at Gibeah, the son of Saul, the Lord's chosen one." And the king said, "I will give them up."»
21.7: The king spared Miphibosheth, son of Jonathan, son of Saul, because of the oath by the Lord that was between them, between David and Jonathan, son of Saul.
21.8: The king took the two sons that Resphah, daughter of Aiah, had borne to Saul, Armoni and Miphibosheth, and the five sons that Michol, daughter of Saul, had borne to Hadriel, son of Bersellai, of Molathi,
21.9: And he delivered them into the hands of the Gibeonites, who hanged them on the mountain before the Lord. All seven perished together; they were put to death in the early days of harvest, at the beginning of the barley harvest.
21.10: Respha, daughter of Aia, having taken a sack, spread it for herself on the rock, from the beginning of harvest until the rain poured down from heaven on them, and she prevented the birds of the sky from landing on them by day and the beasts of the field by night.
21.11: David was told what Respah, daughter of Aiah, Saul's concubine, had done.
21.12: And David went and took the bones of Saul and the bones of Jonathan his son from the inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead, who had taken them from the square of Bethsan, where the Philistines had hung them, on the day that the Philistines had defeated Saul at Gilboa.
21.13: He brought from there the bones of Saul and the bones of Jonathan, his son, and they also gathered the bones of those who had been hanged.
21.14: The bones of Saul and his son Jonathan were buried in the land of Benjamin, at Sela, in the tomb of Zish, Saul's father, and everything the king had commanded was done. After this, God was appeased toward the land.
21.15: There was still war between the Philistines and Israel, and David went down with his servants and they fought against the Philistines: David was tired.
21.16: And Jesbi-Benob, one of the sons of Rapha, whose spear weighed three hundred shekels of bronze and who was girded with a new sword, spoke of striking David.
21.17: Abishai, son of Zeruiah, came to David's aid; he struck down the Philistine and killed him. Then David's men swore an oath to him, saying, "You will no longer go out with us to battle, and you will not quench the torch of Israel."«
21.18: After that, there was another battle at Gob with the Philistines. Then Saboshai the Husathite killed Saph, who was among the sons of Rapha.
21.19: There was another battle at Gob with the Philistines, and Elhanan son of Jaare-Oreghim of Bethlehem killed Goliath of Geth; the shaft of his spear was like the roller of a loom.
21.20: There was yet another battle at Geth. There was a man of great height there, and the fingers of his hands and the toes of his feet numbered six respectively, twenty-four in all, and he too was descended from Rapha.
21.21: He insulted Israel, and Jonathan, son of Shema, David's brother, killed him.
21.22: These four men were sons of Rapha, in Geth; they perished by the hand of David and by the hand of his servants.

22.1: David addressed the words of this song to the Lord on the day when the Lord delivered him from the hand of all his enemies and from the hand of Saul.
22.2: He said: The Lord is my rock, my fortress, my deliverer.
22.3: God is my rock, in whom I find refuge, my shield, the horn of my salvation, my high fortress and my stronghold. My Savior, you have saved me from violence.
22.4: I called upon the one worthy of praise, the Lord, and I was delivered from my enemies.
22.5: For the waves of death surrounded me, the torrents of Belial terrified me.
22.6: The bonds of Sheol entangled me; the nets of death had fallen before me.
22.7: In my distress I called upon the Lord and cried to my God; from his temple he heard my voice, and my cry reached his ears.
22.8: The earth shook and quaked, the foundations of heaven shook and were shaken, because he was angry.,
22.9: Smoke rose from his nostrils and a devouring fire came out of his mouth, from which blazing coals sprang.
22.10: He lowered the heavens and descended; a dark cloud was under his feet.
22.11: He mounted a cherub and flew; he appeared on the wings of the wind.
22.12: He surrounded himself with darkness as with a tent, with pools of water and dark clouds.
22.13: From the brilliance that preceded it sprang embers of fire.
22.14: The Lord thundered from heaven, the Most High made his voice resound.
22.15: He launched arrows and scattered them, and lightning and confounded them.
22.16: Then the bed of the sea appeared, the foundations of the earth were laid bare, at the rebuke of the Lord, at the blast of the wind from his nostrils.
22.17: He reached down from on high and took hold of me; he drew me out of the deep waters,
22.18: He delivered me from my powerful enemy, from those who hated me, even though they were stronger than I.
22.19: They had surprised me in the day of my misfortune, but the Lord was my support.
22.20: He set me free, he saved me, because he took pleasure in me.
22.21: The Lord has rewarded me according to my righteousness; he has repaid me according to the cleanness of my hands.
22.22: For I have kept the ways of the Lord and have not sinned, to stray from my God.
22.23: All his judgments were before me, and I did not deviate from his laws.
22.24: I was blameless towards him and I kept myself on guard against my iniquity.
22.25: The Lord has rewarded me according to my righteousness, according to my purity in his sight.
22.26: With the good man you show yourself good, with the upright man you show yourself upright,
22.27: With the pure, you show yourself to be pure, and with the deceitful, you act treacherously.
22.28: You save the humbled people and with your gaze you humble the proud.
22.29: For you are my light, O Lord, the Lord illuminates my darkness.
22.30: With you I rush upon the armed battalions. With my God I leap over the walls.
22.31: God. His ways are perfect, the word of the Lord is tried and true; he is a shield to all who trust in him.
22.32: For who is God except the Lord, and who is a rock except our God?
22.33: God is my strong fortress; he guides the upright man on his path.
22.34: He makes my feet like those of deer and makes me stand upright on my heights.
22.35: He trains my hands for combat and my arms draw the bronze bow.
22.36: You gave me the shield of your salvation and your pain makes me grow.
22.37: You widen my step beneath me, and my feet do not falter.
22.38: I pursue my enemies and destroy them; I do not return until I have annihilated them.
22.39: I annihilate them, I break them, they do not rise again, they fall beneath my feet.
22.40: You gird me with strength for battle, you make my adversaries bow beneath me.
22.41: You make my enemies turn their backs on me, as you do those who hate me, so that I may exterminate them.
22.42: They look and there is no one to save them. They cry out to the Lord and he does not answer them.
22.43: I grind them like the dust of the earth, like the mud of the streets, I crush them, I trample them.
22.44: You deliver me from the revolts of my people, you preserve me as leader of the nations, a people I did not know are enslaved to me.
22.45: The sons of foreigners flatter me; as soon as they hear, they obey me.
22.46: The sons of foreigners are failing; they are trembling as they emerge from their fortresses.
22.47: The Lord lives and blessed be my rock. God, my rock of refuge, may he be exalted.
22.48: God, who grants me vengeance, who brings peoples down under my feet,
22.49: who delivers me from my enemies, you who raise me above my adversaries, who delivers me from the man of violence.
22.50: Therefore I will praise you among the nations, O Lord, and I will sing to the glory of your name.
22.51: He grants glorious deliverances to his king, he shows mercy to his anointed, to David and to his descendants forever.

23.1: These are the last words of David: The oracle of David, son of Jesse, the oracle of the man on high, the anointed of the God of Jacob, the beloved psalmist of Israel.
23.2: The Spirit of the Lord spoke through me, and his word was on my tongue.
23.3: The God of Israel has spoken, the Rock of Israel has spoken: A righteous one who rules over men, ruling in the fear of God.
23.4: It's like the morning light, when the sun rises on a cloudless morning. Its rays, after the rain, make the grass sprout from the earth.
23.5: Is it not so with my house with God? For he has made with me an everlasting covenant, ordered and kept in every way; yes, he will cause all my salvation and all his good pleasure to spring forth.
23.6: But the people of Belial are all like thorns that are rejected; they cannot be picked up with the hand.,
23.7: The man who touches it arms himself with an iron or wooden spear and they are consumed by fire on the spot.
23.8: These are the names of the heroes who served David: Jesham, son of Hashamoni, the chief of officers. He wielded his spear against eight hundred men, whom he killed at one blow.
23.9: After him was Eleazar, son of Dodo, son of Ahohi. He was among the three valiant men who were with David when they defied the Philistines who had gathered there for battle.,
23.10: While the men of Israel were returning, he rose up and struck down the Philistines until his hand grew weary and clung to the sword. The Lord brought about a great deliverance that day, and the people returned after Eleazar, but only to gather the spoils.
23.11: After him, Shemna, son of Agee the Hararite. The Philistines had gathered together as one band, there was a piece of land full of lentils and the people were fleeing before the Philistines.
23.12: Shemna stood in the middle of the field, defended it, and defeated the Philistines. And the Lord brought about a great victory.
23.13: Three of the thirty captains went down and came to David at harvest time, to the cave of Odollam, while a troop of Philistines was encamped in the valley of Rephaim.
23.14: David was then in the fortress and there was a Philistine outpost in Bethlehem.
23.15: David had a desire and said, «Who will get me water to drink from the cistern that is at the gate of Bethlehem?»
23.16: Immediately the three brave men, passing through the Philistine camp, drew water from the cistern at the gate of Bethlehem. They took it and brought it to David, but he refused to drink it and instead offered it as a libation to the Lord.,
23.17: saying, "Far be it from me, O Lord, to do that. Is this not the blood of those men who went at the peril of their lives?" And he refused to drink it. That is what those three brave men did.
23.18: Abisai, brother of Joab, son of Zeruiah, was also chief of the officers. He raised his spear against three hundred men and killed them, and he became renowned among the three.
23.19: He was the most respected of the three and he was their leader, but he did not equal the first three.
23.20: Benaiah, son of Jehoiada, son of a valiant man of great exploits, from Cabseel. He struck down the two ariels of Moab. He went down and struck down the lion in the middle of the cistern on a snowy day.
23.21: He struck down a fearsome-looking Egyptian, who was holding a spear. He went down to him with a staff, snatched the spear from the Egyptian's hand, and struck him with his own spear.
23.22: This is what Benaiah, son of Jehoiada, did, and he became renowned among the three mighty warriors.
23.23: He was more highly regarded than the thirty, but he did not equal the three. David made him a member of his council.
23.24: Asahel, brother of Joab, was among the thirty, Elhanan son of Dodo of Bethlehem,
23.25: Semma of Harod, Elica of Harod,
23.26: Hélès of Phalti, Hira son of Accès of Thécué,
23.27: Abiéser of Anathoth, Mobonnai the Husatite,
23.28: Selmon the Ahohite, Maharai of Netophah,
23.29: Heled son of Baanah of Netophah, Ethai son of Ribai of Gibeah of the sons of Benjamin,
23.30: Banaiah of Pharaton, Heddai of the valleys of Gaash,
23.31: Abi-Albon of Araba, Azmaveth of Berom,
23.33: Semma the Hararite, Ahiam son of Sarar the Haradite,
23.34: Eliphelet son of Aashbai son of a Machatian, Eliam son of Ahithophel the Gilean,
23.35: Hesrai of Carmel, Pharai of Arbi,
23.36: Igaal son of Nathan of Soba, Bonni of Gad,
23.37: Select the Ammonite, Naharai of Beroth, armor-bearer of Joab son of Zeruiah,
23.38: Ira of Jether, Gareb of Jether,
23.39: Uriah the Hethean. Thirty-seven in all.

24.1: The Lord's anger burned again against Israel, and he incited David against them, saying, "Go, take a census of Israel and Judah."«
24.2: The king said to Joab, the commander of the army, who was with him, «Go through all the tribes of Israel, from Dan to Beersheba, and count the people, so that I may know the number of the people.»
24.3: Joab said to the king, «May the Lord your God increase the people a hundred times what they are now, and may the eyes of my lord the king see it. But why does my lord the king want to do this?»
24.4: But the king's word prevailed against Joab and the commanders of the army, and Joab and the commanders of the army went before the king to take a census of the people of Israel.
24.5: Having crossed the Jordan, they camped at Aroer, to the right of the city, which is in the middle of the valley of Gad, and then at Jazer.
24.6: They came to Gilead and to the land of Tahtim-Hodsi, then they came to Dan-Jaan and to the vicinity of Sidon.
24.7: They came to the stronghold of Tyre and to all the cities of the Hivites and the Canaanites, they reached the Negev of Judah, at Beersheba.
24.8: When they had thus traveled throughout the whole country, they returned to Jerusalem after nine months and twenty days.
24.9: Joab gave the king the census figures: there were eight hundred thousand fighting men in Israel who could wield the sword, and five hundred thousand men in Judah.
24.10: David felt his heart pounding after he had counted the people, and David said to the Lord, «I have sinned greatly in what I have done. Now, O Lord, please take away the iniquity of your servant, for I have acted very foolishly.»
24.11: The next day, when David awoke, the word of the Lord came to Gad the prophet, David's seer, saying:
24.12: «"Go and tell David: This is what the Lord says: I have set three things before you; choose one of them, and I will do it for you."»
24.13: Gad came to David and told him the word of the Lord, saying, «Shall a famine of seven years come upon your land, or shall you flee for three months from your enemies who will pursue you, or shall there be a plague of three days in your land? Now therefore, know and consider what answer I shall give to the one who sent me.»
24.14: David answered Gad, «I am in great distress. Oh, let us fall into the hands of the Lord, for his mercies are great, but let me not fall into the hands of men.»
24.15: And the Lord sent a plague on Israel from the morning of that day until the appointed time, and seventy thousand men of the people died from Dan to Beersheba.
24.16: The angel stretched out his hand over Jerusalem to destroy it. But the Lord relented concerning this disaster and said to the angel who was destroying the people, «Enough! Now withdraw your hand.» The angel of the Lord was standing by the threshing floor of Areunah the Jebusite.
24.17: When David saw the angel striking the people, he said to the Lord, «I am the one who has sinned, I am the one who is guilty, but these sheep, what have they done? Let your hand be on me and on my father’s house.»
24.18: That day Gad came to David and said, «Go up and build an altar to the Lord on the threshing floor of Areunah the Jebusite.»
24.19: David went up, according to the word of Gad, as the Lord had commanded.
24.20: Areuna, having looked, saw the king and his servants heading towards him,
24.21: Areuna went out and bowed down before the king with her face to the ground, saying, «Why has my lord the king come to his servant?» And David answered, «To buy you this threshing floor so that you may build an altar to the Lord, so that the plague may be removed from the people.»
24.22: Areuna said to David, «Let my lord the king take the threshing floor and offer whatever sacrifice seems good to him. Here are the oxen for the burnt offering, the sledges and yokes of the oxen for the wood.
24.23: All this, O king, Areuna gives to the king.» And Areuna also said to the king, «May the Lord your God be gracious to you.»
24.24: But the king said to Areunah, «No. I will buy it from you for money, and I will not offer burnt offerings to the Lord my God that cost me nothing.» So David bought the threshing floor and the oxen for fifty shekels of silver.
24.25: And David built an altar to the Lord there and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings. So the Lord was appeased toward the land, and the plague was averted from Israel.
